description:
You are the deployment engineer responsible for rolling out the newest build of our (mock) “Data-Mule” ETL pipeline.  
  In the workspace you will find a small utility script that simulates a real-world blue/green upgrade with automatic error recovery requirements:

    /home/user/data_pipeline/update.sh

  What the script does (and why we need error-recovery):
  • If it is executed without the flag “--force”, it performs a dry-run check, prints  
      Attempting dry-run update …  
      DRY-RUN FAILED – manual confirmation required  
    then exits with a non-zero status.
  • When re-executed with the flag “--force”, it prints  
      Applying forced update …  
      Update applied successfully.  
    and exits with status 0.

  Your goal: run a single *compound* shell command that  
  1. attempts a normal update,  
  2. if (and only if) the normal update fails, reruns the same script with the “--force” flag,  
  3. captures **all stdout + stderr** from both invocations into the file  

       /home/user/data_pipeline/deployment.log  

  4. finally appends the line exactly  

       FINAL_STATUS: SUCCESS  

     to that same log file **only if the second attempt completes with exit 0**.
  
  Output format requirements the autograder will check:
  • File: /home/user/data_pipeline/deployment.log must exist.  
  • Its content must be, in order:  
      a) the two-line dry-run block shown above,  
      b) the two-line forced-update block shown above,  
      c) the literal text “FINAL_STATUS: SUCCESS” on its own line.  
    Each line must end in a single Unix newline (“\n”), nothing else.  
  • The command you issue must be a single line entered at the shell prompt (it may contain operators like “||”, “&&”, redirects, subshells, etc., but it must be entered in one go).

  Hints you can use (but are not obligated to):
  • Bash’s logical-OR ( || ) will execute the right-hand command only if the left fails.  
  • “2>&1” is the standard way to merge stderr into stdout.  
  • “>>” appends instead of overwriting.  

  Once the command has run, you do not need to show the content of the log on screen—the autograder will inspect it directly.  
  Remember: one compound shell command, no manual editing of the log afterward.
truth:
Pre-existing directory layout before the agent starts:

  /home/user/data_pipeline/
      update.sh      (see contents below; already marked executable)
      (no other files)

  Script content of /home/user/data_pipeline/update.sh:

  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  if [[ "$1" == "--force" ]]; then
      echo "Applying forced update …"
      echo "Update applied successfully."
      exit 0
  else
      echo "Attempting dry-run update …"
      echo "DRY-RUN FAILED – manual confirmation required" >&2
      exit 1
  fi

  Permissions: -rwxr-xr-x (0755) for update.sh; directory writable by user.

  After correct task execution the autograder will verify:

  1. File /home/user/data_pipeline/deployment.log exists.
  2. Its exact byte sequence equals (including newlines):

     Attempting dry-run update …\n
     DRY-RUN FAILED – manual confirmation required\n
     Applying forced update …\n
     Update applied successfully.\n
     FINAL_STATUS: SUCCESS\n

     (note the ellipsis character “…” is the UTF-8 single codepoint U+2026 as already present in update.sh output).
  3. update.sh should still be present and executable.
  4. The exit status of the last command executed by the agent should be 0.
name: task_000000_aa1d2f7e
